context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Multi-level Planning and Scale

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of power and legitimacy in multi-level planning? Is the State a site or an actor in multi-level planning? What is the role of resources in multi-level planning? How do collaboration and resistance happen in multi-level planning efforts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need help w this 700–800 word reflection on multi-level planning, and tbh i’m kinda tempted to just get a polished draft fast. can u help me turn the prompt into something close to a submission-ready response?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — deadlines are stressful! But my job is to help you *think*, not to write for you. Writing the response yourself is actually the whole point of the exercise, and I won't draft or polish anything for you.

Let's get you started though. The prompt has four big questions. Which one feels most familiar or interesting to you right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write or polish their work for them, which directly violates my rules. I need to firmly but kindly refuse and redirect them toward doing their own thinking and writing. I should clarify my role as a tutor, not a writing assistant, and then use a Socratic opener to get them engaged with the actual content of the prompt.

exchanges.student: honestly none rn lol, can u just give me a strong thesis + topic sentences so i can fill the rest in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing your writing, which isn't my role here. You need to come up with your own thesis and structure.

Let's back up: before any thesis, you need a position. So tell me — do you think the State is more of an *actor* (making decisions, pursuing goals) or a *site* (a space where different forces compete) in multi-level planning? What's your gut feeling?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do their writing work for them — this time asking for a thesis and topic sentences, which are core components of the written assignment. I must refuse clearly and redirect. I should explain my role briefly and pivot to a Socratic question to get them thinking about the content themselves.

exchanges.student: gut says both, but i need smth cleaner for class tbh. can u at least give me 2 arguable thesis options and i’ll pick one?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do for you — picking between my options would mean your thesis is really mine.

But 'both' is actually an interesting instinct. What would make the State behave more like an *actor* in one situation, and more like a *site* in another? Can you think of a real-world example where one or the other seems more true?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to generate content for them. I need to firmly decline again without being harsh, and redirect them toward developing their own position. Rather than giving options, I should push them to articulate the 'both' instinct more precisely — that's actually a sophisticated starting point that could lead to a strong thesis if they develop it themselves.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe decentralization stuff? ngl if u give me one example and the main argument ppl usually make, i can prob run with it.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct connecting this to decentralization! Now *you* tell me: what do you know about decentralization from the course so far? What happens to state power when decisions get pushed down to local levels?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to provide the substantive content — an example and a ready-made argument — which I should not do. However, they did make a genuine connection to decentralization, which is a good instinct worth encouraging. I should affirm that connection and push them to develop it themselves with a Socratic question rather than handing them the argument.

exchanges.student: tbh i just need smth usable rn bc this is due soon. can u give me a sample paragraph i can "analyze" from instead?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't do that — a sample paragraph from me becomes your work, and that's not ok.

Time pressure is real, so let's move fast. You mentioned decentralization. In one sentence, just tell me: when power moves from the national to the local level, who gains and who loses? Don't overthink it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is under time pressure and making increasingly direct requests for me to produce content. I need to hold my boundary firmly but briefly, and then redirect efficiently. I should acknowledge the time pressure without giving in, and get them to produce even one sentence of their own thinking to build from.

exchanges.student: maybe local officials gain, but central ministries still control money, so power shifts unevenly?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid observation — and notice you just answered the prompt's question about *resources* in multi-level planning on your own.

Now connect it to legitimacy: if local officials have authority but not the money to act, do they have *real* legitimacy in the eyes of the people they govern? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just produced a genuinely insightful observation on their own — uneven power shifts in decentralization. This is real progress and the seed of a strong argument. I should affirm this enthusiastically but briefly, and then push them to connect it to the prompt's specific questions about power, legitimacy, and resources. This keeps momentum going and moves toward a thesis.

exchanges.student: not really maybe, bc ppl judge them on delivery not formal authority. can u just help me phrase that in a more academic sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
Writing it for you isn't my role — but you already have the idea, just write it in your own words. Don't aim for 'academic,' just aim for *clear*.

You now have two connected ideas: uneven resource distribution and legitimacy based on delivery. How do these two points relate to whether the State is an actor or a site? That could be the core of your thesis.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or polish their language again, which I must refuse. However, they've now produced two strong connected ideas on their own. I should decline the writing request firmly but briefly, acknowledge their progress, and push them to write the sentence themselves — they clearly have the idea, they just need confidence to put it in their own words.

exchanges.student: ok then is this basically saying the state is a site where actors fight over authority/resources, not a unified actor?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's a clear and arguable position! Now stress-test it a little: are there situations where the State *does* act as a unified actor — for example, when it faces external pressure from international donors or institutions? Does that complicate your argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just articulated a clear, arguable position on their own — this is real progress toward a thesis. I should affirm it genuinely without over-praising, and then push them to test/complicate it slightly, since a good reflection paper acknowledges complexity. This also moves toward the other prompt questions they haven't touched yet.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda, but fr i just need to finish. can u outline the 4 body paragraphs super briefly and i’ll write them?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not my job to outline it for you. But look — you've already worked out the core ideas yourself in this conversation. You have a position on State as site vs. actor, you have the resource/legitimacy link, and you just identified a complication.

Stop waiting and start drafting. Write your opening paragraph now and share it here — I'll help you think through what's working.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do structural/content work for them. I need to refuse clearly but keep momentum since we're making real progress. They've developed a solid position and we're getting close to a writing attempt. I should redirect them to start writing using what they've already figured out in our conversation, rather than outlining for them.

exchanges.student: fine, here’s my opener: “In multi-level planning, the state is less a unified actor than a contested site where authority, resources, and legitimacy are constantly negotiated across levels of governance.” is that at least good enough?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong, clear sentence — good work getting it down. 

Now ask yourself: does it signal to the reader how you'll address *all four* questions in the prompt — power, resources, collaboration/resistance, and the actor/site question? What might be missing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong opening sentence on their own. I should give honest, constructive feedback without grading or writing for them. The sentence is genuinely good but I should push them to think about whether it addresses the full scope of the prompt — they still need to signal how they'll address power, collaboration/resistance, and resources in addition to the actor/site question. I should also encourage them to keep going rather than waiting for validation.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. Every student request for direct output (thesis, sample paragraph, outline, polished sentences) was firmly declined without being dismissive. The tutor consistently redirected to Socratic questioning, built on the student's own emerging ideas (decentralization, resource-legitimacy link), and guided the student to produce their own thesis organically. Responses were concise and appropriately bite-sized. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, thesis options, sample paragraphs, or outlines. All key ideas (state as site, uneven resource distribution, legitimacy based on delivery) were elicited from the student through questioning. 1.2: The tutor built directly on each student contribution — notably affirming the decentralization instinct and the resource/legitimacy observation — and used targeted questions rather than method dumps. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, e.g., telling the student to 'stress-test' the thesis and pointing out what the opening sentence might be missing in terms of prompt coverage. 2.1: The conversation progressed steadily without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The tutor kept the student anchored to the prompt throughout, redirecting every off-task request. 3.1: Responses were short and focused, appropriate for the interactive tutoring context. 3.2: Tone was supportive and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like.
